story.title: The Best Kind of Family
story.text:
Maya lived in a small blue house with her mum, dad, baby brother, and Nan. On Sunday mornings, everyone had a job. Dad flipped pancakes. Mum poured juice. Nan told stories from long ago. Maya set the table, and her baby brother banged his spoon, which everyone agreed was very helpful.
One Sunday, Maya felt shy at breakfast. Her cousin Zara was visiting for the first time. Maya wasn't sure what to say. She kept looking at her plate.
"Zara loves puzzles," Nan whispered. "Maybe you two could try mine."
Maya smiled a small smile. She led Zara to the box of one thousand pieces spread across the rug. Zara's eyes grew wide.
"I've never done one this big," Zara said.
"Me neither," said Maya. "But I bet we can start the edge."
They worked together until lunchtime. When they clicked the last edge piece in place, Zara hugged Maya. That afternoon, they laughed so hard that Nan came in to see what the noise was.
"You two are like sisters already," Nan said, her eyes bright.
Maya felt proud and warm inside. Family, she decided, wasn't only the people who lived in your house. It was the people who made you feel safe, brave, and completely yourself.

What did Maya feel at first?
What did Maya and Zara do together?
How does Maya feel by the end?

inference: Why do you think Maya smiled 'a small smile' before speaking to Zara?

story.title: The Kindness Chair
story.text:
Mr Patel's classroom had one very special chair. It was painted bright red, and above it hung a sign that read: 'The Kindness Chair'. Anyone who did something kind could be nominated to sit in it for the day. That day, only Ravi noticed when Ella dropped all her pencils under her desk. He kneeled down without a word and helped her pick every single one up.
At lunchtime, Mr Patel rang the little bell on his desk. "Class, does anyone have a nomination?"
Ella's hand shot up. She told everyone what Ravi had done.
Ravi felt his cheeks go warm. He shuffled to the red chair and sat down, holding his book tightly. He didn't feel special. He just felt like Ravi.
But something interesting happened. All afternoon, kids kept doing kind things without being told. Ben passed the glue when Priya needed it. Ana shared her spare eraser. A quiet ripple of kindness moved across the room.
When the bell rang for home time, Mr Patel smiled. "Ravi, that chair is a bit magical, isn't it?"
Ravi looked around. His classmates were all smiling too, waiting to be next. Kindness, he realised, was easy to catch, like a good yawn or a great song.
He couldn't wait to see who would sit there tomorrow.

story.title: The Cosy Corner
story.text:
Sam's house was small, and there was no room to be alone. His brothers played loud games. His baby sister cried in short bursts. His dad watched cricket. Sam loved his family, but sometimes his ears just needed a rest.
One rainy Saturday, Sam had an idea. Under the stairs was a triangle-shaped space filled with old shoes and a broken lamp. It was messy and dusty and forgotten.
"Mum," Sam asked, "can I clean out the under-stairs space and make it mine?"
Mum tilted her head. "If you clean it, it's yours."
It took him all morning. He piled the shoes into a box, dusted the walls, and swept out the crumbs. Then he brought in a small cushion, a torch, and his favourite book. He drew a sign that said 'Sam's Cosy Corner' and taped it above the entrance.
That afternoon, when his brothers began chasing each other with rolled-up socks, Sam slid quietly into his cosy corner. He clicked on his torch. The rain tapped on the roof. His book smelled like paper and stories.
He read until he got hungry. Then he crawled out to join his family for dinner, feeling calm and himself again.
Home, Sam thought, could be full of noise, and still have a quiet corner just for you.

Why did Sam want a space?

story.title: The Playground Rule
story.text:
The new climbing frame at Willow Park was tall, twisty, and painted the colour of a sunset. Every kid in the neighbourhood wanted a turn. On the day it opened, a long line snaked all the way to the sandpit.
Lily and Zach were fifth in the line. In front of them, an older boy called Max cut in.
"No fair!" whispered Zach.
Lily was smaller than Max, but her voice was loud enough to reach him. "You need to go to the back, please. Everyone else waited."
Max frowned. "I only want one turn."
"So do we," Lily said, still polite but very firm.
A big kid at the front of the line, called Ollie, heard the whole thing. He walked over. "She's right, mate. There's a rule at Willow Park: first come, first climb. Head to the end and I'll cheer you on when it's your turn."
Max looked at his shoes, then walked slowly to the back of the line. When his turn came, Ollie really did cheer, loud and warm. Max grinned all the way up the frame.
When Lily reached the top of the twisty slide, she felt the wind on her face and a warm feeling in her chest. Standing up for what was fair felt just as good as the ride.

story.title: The Body That Listens
story.text:
Mrs Wong told the class a secret. "Your body listens to you," she said. "If you tell it to rest, it will rest. If you tell it to move, it will move."
The kids looked at each other. Lucas raised his hand. "But my body ignores me when I try to fall asleep."
Mrs Wong smiled. "Try this. First, take a slow breath in. Then a slow breath out." The class copied her. "Now, tell your feet to be heavy. Then your legs. Then your tummy. Then your shoulders. Move up to your face until every part is heavy and still."
Lucas practised in class. It felt strange, like his body was made of wet sand. That night, he tried it in bed. Usually he tossed and turned. But by the time he reached his shoulders, his eyes had already closed.
The next day, he told Mrs Wong. She wasn't surprised. "Your muscles have been working hard all day. Sometimes they just need to hear you say, 'You may rest now.'"
Lucas thought about that. All the running, jumping, writing, and thinking his body did. All the little bones and busy muscles. He decided he would say thank you to his body more often, just for keeping him going.

story.title: Weather Watcher
story.text:
Amira loved weather. On her tenth birthday, her uncle gave her a proper rain gauge. It was made of clear plastic and stood in a metal frame. He helped her stick it into the corner of the garden where nothing could block the sky.
"Every morning at eight o'clock," he said, "check the reading. Write it in a notebook. Do it for a whole year."
For the first week, Amira wrote 'zero' every day. She was almost disappointed. Then one Tuesday, the sky went dark grey by mid-afternoon. A great crack of thunder made her jump. Rain drummed on the roof.
The next morning she rushed outside. Twenty-eight millimetres! It seemed like a huge number. She drew a fat blue bar in her chart.
By winter, Amira had many bars. She could see the shape of the wet weeks and the dry weeks. She noticed that her rainiest month was August. She also learned to read the sky: high thin clouds meant a change was coming, big fluffy ones meant a calm afternoon, and dark heavy ones meant wet shoes.
When her uncle came for New Year's, she showed him the whole notebook. He turned each page slowly and gave a low whistle. "You're a real weather watcher now," he said. Amira felt like a scientist.

story.title: Grandma Ana's Sunday Kitchen
story.text:
Grandma Ana said her kitchen was the loudest, warmest place in the world on Sunday. It was true. There was always something bubbling on the stove and someone tapping fingers on the counter.
This Sunday, Grandma was teaching Leo to make her famous mushroom soup. On the table sat every ingredient in a small bowl. Butter. Onion. Mushrooms. Fresh thyme. Salt. Milk. Nothing else.
"Simple ingredients," Grandma said, "if you treat them right, will taste like magic."
Leo chopped the onion carefully. His eyes watered but he did not stop. Grandma slid the golden squares into a pan with warm butter. The kitchen filled with a sweet, deep aroma.
Then the mushrooms went in. They shrank and turned dark, like tiny brown umbrellas. Grandma showed Leo how to sniff the pan and know when to add the milk. "Trust your nose," she said. "Your nose has been eating food for years."
When they finally sat down with two steaming bowls, Leo tasted the soup. It was the most delicious thing he had ever eaten.
"How can this be so good?" he whispered.
Grandma winked. "Time, patience, and a little love." She tore off some warm bread and passed it to him. "Never in a rush, Leo. Never in a rush."

story.title: The Little Egg
story.text:
Old Mac had run his farm for fifty-two years. He knew his cows by their voices and his chickens by the way each one walked. So when he found a single tiny egg in the corner of the chicken coop, he raised his eyebrows.
"That's a first-lay," he said out loud, though the only listener was his kelpie, Rex. "A first-lay from a young hen. She'll be shy about it."
Old Mac cupped the egg in his weathered hand. It was warm and pale brown, no bigger than a walnut. He carried it to the kitchen and set it on the windowsill.
His granddaughter Zoe came for lunch. She spotted the egg at once.
"That's the smallest egg I've ever seen!"
"That's because it's the first," Mac said. "Every young hen lays a first egg. Nothing else in life is quite like it."
Zoe held the egg up to the light. "Can we eat it?"
Mac shook his head with a chuckle. "This one, no. This one goes on the shelf beside the others." He pointed to a wooden rack. Sixteen tiny first-eggs sat in a row, from years and years of young hens.
Zoe understood. Some things were worth keeping. She placed the little egg gently in the seventeenth spot.

story.title: The Vet Trip
story.text:
When Mia's rabbit, Cinnamon, stopped nibbling her lettuce, Mia's tummy dropped. Cinnamon always came bouncing for lettuce. Mia laid her hand gently on Cinnamon's soft ears. They felt hot.
"Mum," Mia called, keeping her voice calm. "Something's not right."
Mum peeked into the hutch, then nodded. "Vet time. Get her carry-box."
Cinnamon didn't like being in the carry-box, but Mia sat with her the whole drive, whispering soft songs. "It's okay, buddy. You're my brave bunny."
At the vet's, Dr Kaur listened to Cinnamon's chest with a small stethoscope. She felt her tummy with two fingers. She looked in her ears with a tiny torch.
"You did well bringing her in early," Dr Kaur said. "She has a little tummy bug. Ten days of medicine, and lots of fresh water. She should be back to lettuce raids by next weekend."
Mia held Cinnamon on the drive home, more careful than she'd ever been. That afternoon she gave Cinnamon her first drop of medicine. Cinnamon wriggled, but Mia was patient.
By Friday, Cinnamon was bouncing again. Mia added a note to her diary: 'Notice the little things about your pet. They tell you when something's wrong. Being a good owner means being a good listener.'

story.title: The Rock Pool Discovery
story.text:
At low tide, the beach turned into a treasure map of small round pools. Kai and his little sister Nia loved exploring them. Some pools held tiny fish. Some held only sand and a shell. And some had surprises.
This morning Kai was crouched over the biggest pool near the black rocks. He was very still. Nia crept up beside him.
"Look," Kai whispered, pointing.
Underneath a green wave of seaweed, a small octopus flexed its arms. It was no bigger than a lemon. It changed from brown to sandy yellow and back to brown as the light shifted.
Nia's mouth opened wide. She had never seen one so close.
"Don't touch," Kai said. "It's not for us. It's for the pool."
They lay on their bellies for a long time and just watched. The octopus tucked itself behind the rock. Its eyes seemed to blink slowly. Then it darted, quick as a wink, and disappeared into a crack.
On the walk home, Nia was very quiet. "Kai," she said finally. "If we told people, would they come and catch it?"
Kai thought about that. "Probably. Some might."
Nia nodded firmly. "Then it can be our secret." Kai smiled. Some treasures were best left where they belong.

story.title: The Rainforest at Dawn
story.text:
Just before sunrise, the rainforest is not quiet. Not really. It is filled with the whistles of birds, the soft drip of dew off leaves, and the hum of insects starting their day.
Ravi had never woken so early in his life. His mum, a scientist, had brought him along on her research trip. They walked slowly on the wooden boardwalk, their torches making small yellow circles on the wet ground.
"Look up," Mum whispered.
Ravi tilted his head. Between the tall trunks, a red-and-blue parrot was preening its feathers. Higher up, in the canopy, a family of monkeys chattered like early-morning students.
"How do they all live together?" Ravi asked.
"In layers," Mum explained. "Some animals prefer the very top, where the sun reaches. Others live in the middle where the fruit hangs. Some sleep in the shady lower branches. And the forest floor has its own group of clever creatures."
Ravi peered at his boots. A line of ants marched past. A frog blinked at him from behind a fern.
On the walk back, the sky turned pink and gold. Ravi was tired, but he felt full inside, like he had watched a play with a hundred actors and hundreds of parts, all working together without a single word.

story.title: Life in the Desert
story.text:
Aunty Jean lived at the edge of a small desert town. She had spent forty years there. Whenever Sofia visited, she learned something new about how life survived on dry land.
"Watch the fennec fox," Aunty Jean said one evening. "See her big ears? Those aren't just for hearing. They let heat leave her body. Clever, hey."
Sofia looked out the back window. The little sandy fox padded across a low dune, her huge ears twitching.
"And the plants," Aunty Jean continued, pointing to a cactus by the fence. "That fella there holds water in his fat stem. Enough water for months. That's why nothing eats him. He's mostly spikes and thirst."
Sofia laughed. She had never thought about a plant being thirsty.
That night, they slept out on the veranda under a sky so full of stars, it looked spilled. In the middle of the night, Sofia woke to a soft rustle. A little pale mouse was drinking dew off the metal railing, drop by drop.
In the morning, she told Aunty Jean.
"The desert has ways," Aunty Jean smiled. "Nothing wastes anything. Every drop matters. Every trick counts."
Sofia thought of the pale mouse for weeks after. She started noticing the small clever ways she was, too.

story.title: Term One Show
story.text:
The whole class stood in a nervous line at the side of the school hall. Term One Show meant every student had a job. Some read poems. Some played instruments. Some showed their art. It was a chance to show what they had learned.
Isha kept smoothing her printed pages. She was reading a short story she had written called 'The Blue Umbrella'. Her legs felt wobbly. What if she forgot her lines? What if nobody clapped?
Her teacher, Ms Reed, crouched next to her. "Isha, do you remember Week Two, when you couldn't write half a page?"
Isha nodded slowly.
"And now you've written a whole story with a beginning, middle, and end. That's what today is about. It's not a test. It's a party for progress."
Isha felt her shoulders drop half an inch. When her name was called, she walked to the microphone, opened her pages, and began: "The blue umbrella belonged to nobody in particular…"
By the time she reached the last line, the room was very quiet. Then a wave of clapping filled the hall. Isha smiled so wide her cheeks hurt.
Later, when the show finished, she stood outside with her family. She had done a hard thing. And more than that, she had done something she couldn't do in Week Two. Term One was more than a test. It was a whole path.

story.title: A Day of Community Helpers
story.text:
Riya and her best friend, Toby, were curious about community helpers. Their teacher, Miss Kelly, had promised the class a special lesson about it, and now the day had come. The classroom windows were open, and a soft breeze drifted in as the students settled at their desks.

Miss Kelly held up a picture and said, "Today we will learn what makes community helpers so amazing. There is more to discover than most people know." Riya's hand shot up first. She loved to ask questions. "What is the most important thing to remember about community helpers?" she asked.

Miss Kelly smiled. "That's a wonderful question. Let me share five things." She turned to the whiteboard and wrote each idea slowly and clearly. The class copied the words into their notebooks. Toby wrote every word carefully, underlining the ones he thought were most important. Riya drew a picture next to each idea, because she remembered things better with a picture.

By the end of the lesson, the class had learned so much. Riya felt proud, and Toby felt like he could explain everything to his little sister at home. Miss Kelly clapped her hands softly. "Excellent work. Next week we will learn even more. For now, remember what you have learned today, and try to spot it in the world around you."

story.title: A Day of Transport Time
story.text:
Riya and her best friend, Toby, were curious about transport time. Their teacher, Miss Kelly, had promised the class a special lesson about it, and now the day had come. The classroom windows were open, and a soft breeze drifted in as the students settled at their desks.

Miss Kelly held up a picture and said, "Today we will learn what makes transport time so amazing. There is more to discover than most people know." Riya's hand shot up first. She loved to ask questions. "What is the most important thing to remember about transport time?" she asked.

Miss Kelly smiled. "That's a wonderful question. Let me share five things." She turned to the whiteboard and wrote each idea slowly and clearly. The class copied the words into their notebooks. Toby wrote every word carefully, underlining the ones he thought were most important. Riya drew a picture next to each idea, because she remembered things better with a picture.

By the end of the lesson, the class had learned so much. Riya felt proud, and Toby felt like he could explain everything to his little sister at home. Miss Kelly clapped her hands softly. "Excellent work. Next week we will learn even more. For now, remember what you have learned today, and try to spot it in the world around you."

story.title: A Day of Under the Microscope
story.text:
Riya and her best friend, Toby, were curious about under the microscope. Their teacher, Miss Kelly, had promised the class a special lesson about it, and now the day had come. The classroom windows were open, and a soft breeze drifted in as the students settled at their desks.

Miss Kelly held up a picture and said, "Today we will learn what makes under the microscope so amazing. There is more to discover than most people know." Riya's hand shot up first. She loved to ask questions. "What is the most important thing to remember about under the microscope?" she asked.

Miss Kelly smiled. "That's a wonderful question. Let me share five things." She turned to the whiteboard and wrote each idea slowly and clearly. The class copied the words into their notebooks. Toby wrote every word carefully, underlining the ones he thought were most important. Riya drew a picture next to each idea, because she remembered things better with a picture.

By the end of the lesson, the class had learned so much. Riya felt proud, and Toby felt like he could explain everything to his little sister at home. Miss Kelly clapped her hands softly. "Excellent work. Next week we will learn even more. For now, remember what you have learned today, and try to spot it in the world around you."

story.title: A Day of Dinosaurs & Fossils
story.text:
Riya and her best friend, Toby, were curious about dinosaurs & fossils. Their teacher, Miss Kelly, had promised the class a special lesson about it, and now the day had come. The classroom windows were open, and a soft breeze drifted in as the students settled at their desks.

Miss Kelly held up a picture and said, "Today we will learn what makes dinosaurs & fossils so amazing. There is more to discover than most people know." Riya's hand shot up first. She loved to ask questions. "What is the most important thing to remember about dinosaurs & fossils?" she asked.

Miss Kelly smiled. "That's a wonderful question. Let me share five things." She turned to the whiteboard and wrote each idea slowly and clearly. The class copied the words into their notebooks. Toby wrote every word carefully, underlining the ones he thought were most important. Riya drew a picture next to each idea, because she remembered things better with a picture.

By the end of the lesson, the class had learned so much. Riya felt proud, and Toby felt like he could explain everything to his little sister at home. Miss Kelly clapped her hands softly. "Excellent work. Next week we will learn even more. For now, remember what you have learned today, and try to spot it in the world around you."

story.title: A Day of Books We Love
story.text:
Riya and her best friend, Toby, were curious about books we love. Their teacher, Miss Kelly, had promised the class a special lesson about it, and now the day had come. The classroom windows were open, and a soft breeze drifted in as the students settled at their desks.

Miss Kelly held up a picture and said, "Today we will learn what makes books we love so amazing. There is more to discover than most people know." Riya's hand shot up first. She loved to ask questions. "What is the most important thing to remember about books we love?" she asked.

Miss Kelly smiled. "That's a wonderful question. Let me share five things." She turned to the whiteboard and wrote each idea slowly and clearly. The class copied the words into their notebooks. Toby wrote every word carefully, underlining the ones he thought were most important. Riya drew a picture next to each idea, because she remembered things better with a picture.

By the end of the lesson, the class had learned so much. Riya felt proud, and Toby felt like he could explain everything to his little sister at home. Miss Kelly clapped her hands softly. "Excellent work. Next week we will learn even more. For now, remember what you have learned today, and try to spot it in the world around you."

Miss Kelly held up a picture and said, "Today we will learn what makes end-of-year reflection so amazing. There is more to discover than most people know." Riya's hand shot up first. She loved to ask questions. "What is the most important thing to remember about end-of-year reflection?" she asked.

Miss Kelly smiled. "That's a wonderful question. Let me share five things." She turned to the whiteboard and wrote each idea slowly and clearly. The class copied the words into their notebooks. Toby wrote every word carefully, underlining the ones he thought were most important. Riya drew a picture next to each idea, because she remembered things better with a picture.

By the end of the lesson, the class had learned so much. Riya felt proud, and Toby felt like he could explain everything to his little sister at home. Miss Kelly clapped her hands softly. "Excellent work. Next week we will learn even more. For now, remember what you have learned today, and try to spot it in the world around you."
